sample.name: at.zip
sample.magic_description: Zip archive data, at least v5.1 to extract, compression method=AES Encrypted

failed.error: Hybrid Analysis API error 403: {"message":"Access denied. This operation requires 'default' privileges."}

early_findings: ZIP extracted: 1 file(s); primary='bc37921377b4fe391a8487b7116cb8b92b3b09b1c0e9b4f48fb99f217f43eec2.exe' re-routed as 'pe'; password='infected'

file_info.true_type: .NET managed executable (PE32, imports only _CorExeMain from mscoree.dll)
file_info.packer: Unknown — no DIE output provided; high entropy and near-identical .text section entropy strongly suggest packing or encryption of the managed assembly, or a self-contained single-section .NET bundle

pe_info.compile_timestamp_note: Timestamp is ~33 years in the future; almost certainly forged. Treat as meaningless for dating the build.

sections.name: .text
sections.notes: Virtual size and raw size are essentially equal (~983 KB each), so there is no classic packer gap. However, entropy of 7.87 is extremely high for .NET IL — legitimate managed assemblies typically sit between 5.0 and 6.5. This either means the assembly is itself compressed/encrypted (e.g., a ConfuserEx-protected or bundled single-file publish), or the .text section contains a packed native stub that wraps a managed payload.

sections.name: .rsrc
sections.notes: Small resource section with moderate entropy. Consistent with a minimal icon or version resource — nothing anomalous.

sections.name: .reloc
sections.notes: Tiny relocation table, near-zero entropy. Normal for .NET executables.

api_calls.notes: The IAT contains only this single export from mscoree.dll — the standard .NET runtime entry point. All actual functionality is implemented in managed IL (or obfuscated/compressed managed code) inside .text. Native API usage cannot be assessed from the IAT alone; any P/Invoke or reflection-based calls would not appear here.

locale_artifacts: Multiple French-language strings present: 'Fermer' (Close), 'Jardin de Mousse' (Moss Garden), 'Plantez vos espèces, gérez les saisons' (Plant your species, manage the seasons), 'Exposition' (Exhibition), 'À Propos' (About), 'Crédits' (Credits). Locale suggests French-speaking developer or deliberately French-themed application.

evidence: .text section entropy: 7.87 on ~984 KB of content
IAT contains only _CorExeMain — no native API surface visible
correlations.conclusion: The combination of near-maximal entropy across the entire dominant section, a minimal IAT, and a forged timestamp is a strong indicator that the .NET assembly has been deliberately obfuscated or compressed. This is consistent with tools such as ConfuserEx, .NET Reactor, Eazfuscator, or a single-file publish with compression — all of which produce high-entropy output and suppress readable strings/API calls at the PE layer. The true capability set is hidden inside the managed bytecode and cannot be read without decompilation or dynamic observation.

evidence: Unicode strings referencing 'Jardin de Mousse' (Moss Garden simulation)
Strings: 'Plantez vos espèces, gérez les saisons, observez le développement de votre écosystème miniature'
UI control names: FormAPropos, FormCredits, btnFermer, lblTitre, lblDescription, lblCredits
correlations.conclusion: A coherent set of UI strings describes a French-language Windows Forms application themed around growing a Japanese moss garden. The attribution string 'Antigravity (Google DeepMind Team)' is notable: it either (a) is a genuine developer handle or team name, or (b) is a false flag intended to mislead attribution. The application claims to be a .NET 4.5 WinForms program. These strings survived obfuscation, suggesting they are resource strings embedded in a way the obfuscator did not encrypt, or the obfuscation is partial.

evidence: Readable UI/application strings present in Unicode section
Entropy of .text at 7.87 — inconsistent with plain managed IL which would produce lower entropy
correlations.conclusion: The coexistence of readable French UI strings alongside extreme entropy is somewhat contradictory. Two explanations: (1) the strings are stored in the low-entropy .rsrc section or in uncompressed metadata while the IL itself is compressed/encrypted; or (2) only the code sections are obfuscated while string resources are left plain. This partial-obfuscation pattern is characteristic of ConfuserEx with string-encryption disabled or of a packer that wraps an otherwise normal application. Dynamic analysis or managed decompilation (dnSpy/ILSpy after unpacking) is required to resolve this.

Format strings referencing latitude, longitude, delta-latitude, delta-longitude, direction, altitude, and index are incongruous with a purely cosmetic moss-garden application. These could be: (a) remnant debug/logging strings from flight-simulation or navigation code; (b) functionality related to geolocation of the victim machine; or (c) part of a C2 protocol encoding position data. This is a low-confidence but notable anomaly that warrants close attention during dynamic analysis and decompilation.

static_analysis.behavior_hypothesis: This is a .NET 4.5 x86 Windows GUI application that presents itself as a French-language moss-garden botanical simulation ('Jardin de Mousse') attributed to 'Antigravity (Google DeepMind Team)'. The entire executable is heavily obfuscated or packed — most likely with a .NET obfuscator (ConfuserEx or similar) — producing near-maximal entropy across the .text section and suppressing all native API visibility. The forged year-2057 compile timestamp confirms deliberate anti-forensics. The true behavior is unknown without decompilation: it could be a benign hobbyist application with aggressive obfuscation, or a malicious payload using a moss-garden theme as a lure. The geolocation-flavored format strings (Lat, Lon, Direction, Altitude) are anomalous for a gardening app and raise the possibility of location-reporting or navigation-related functionality hidden beneath the surface.

static_verdict.type: Unknown — obfuscated .NET application; cannot exclude dropper, infostealer, or lure without decompilation
static_verdict.justification: Heavy obfuscation (entropy 7.87, forged timestamp, minimal IAT) prevents static API and code-path analysis; the only visible content is French UI strings consistent with a botanical simulation and anomalous geolocation format strings. Malicious intent is plausible but unconfirmed.

to_confirm_dynamically: Identify the obfuscation/packing layer and whether a secondary payload is dropped or reflectively loaded at runtime
Capture all .NET P/Invoke, reflection, and Assembly.Load calls — these will reveal the true native API surface hidden from the IAT
Monitor network activity: do the Lat/Lon/Direction format strings appear in any outbound request (DNS, HTTP, raw socket)?
Check whether the application contacts any remote host (C2 beaconing, exfiltration) during or after displaying the UI
Observe file-system and registry writes — persistence mechanisms would not be visible statically given the obfuscation
Determine whether 'Jardin de Mousse' UI is actually rendered, or whether it is a decoy that runs only briefly before malicious code executes
Investigate the 'Antigravity (Google DeepMind Team)' attribution — is it a real developer handle or a false flag?
Confirm .NET runtime version actually used at execution (claimed: .NET 4.5)
missing_inputs: DIE (Detect-It-Easy) output — packer/obfuscator identification could narrow the unpacking approach
FLOSS decoded strings — obfuscated .NET assemblies may yield decoded stack strings that FLOSS can recover; not provided
Full strings output — truncated at 50/type out of 10,353 ASCII and 389 Unicode; additional strings (especially Unicode) may contain URLs, registry keys, mutex names, or additional format strings
Resource section details — .rsrc contents (icons, version info, embedded blobs) not enumerated
Managed metadata — .NET assembly manifest, type/method names, referenced assemblies (would require ildasm or dnSpy output)
Authenticode signature information — not provided; signed samples change the risk profile

file_reputation.detection_rate: 35/74 (47%) — moderate-to-high; 34 undetected suggests fresh/recently-compiled sample or targeted operation
family_labels: Gen:Variant.MSILHeracles.250383 (BitDefender, MicroWorld-eScan, ALYac, VIPRE, Emsisoft, GData, Arcabit)
related_samples: Same imphash (f34d5f2d4577ed6d9ceec516c1f5a744) clusters this with .NET-only executables that import solely _CorExeMain from mscoree.dll — a standard .NET stub imphash shared across many .NET samples and not a unique pivot
vhash 295036751513c0822928501c may identify a cluster in VT
file_reputation.imphash_cluster: f34d5f2d4577ed6d9ceec516c1f5a744 — standard .NET-stub imphash; not uniquely identifying; shared by all .NET PE executables that import only mscoree!_CorExeMain

string_research.finding: Appears as both the PE version-info 'product' field and 'description' field. No prior threat-intelligence reporting found associating the string 'MothGarden' with a known malware family or threat actor. The name appears purpose-built as a lure/decoy label for this sample; its absence from OSINT suggests a novel or highly targeted campaign. The botanical/nature theme (moth + garden) is consistent with the French-language 'Jardin de Mousse' (moss garden) lure noted in static analysis.

Assembly name (meaningful_name) as reported by VT. Also listed as an alternative submission filename. Short, randomised-looking name consistent with obfuscated/generated output from ConfuserEx or similar packers. No prior OSINT association found.

Used as both the Run-key registry value name (HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\qlQjUq) and as a filename (qlqjuq.exe dropped to %APPDATA%). The mixed-case, pseudo-random pattern is consistent with auto-generated persistence key names common to Agent Tesla and MSILHeracles-family loaders/droppers. No standalone public reporting on this specific string found, reinforcing novelty of this sample.
string_research.reference: VT sigma_analysis_results; VT names[]; static analysis

string_research.finding: Top-level .NET namespace visible in the type-definition list. Short, opaque namespace name is typical of obfuscated .NET assemblies post-ConfuserEx processing. Not uniquely attributable.

Persistence mechanism observed via Sigma rule match: Run-key value pointing to a hidden PowerShell script in %TEMP% with randomised filenames (5w0dcx2ub4o.ps1, j2zml5z1viu.ps1, rmeg2mcvj2n.ps1, qtj5s13mntr.ps1). This pattern — PowerShell-based stage-2 launcher written to %TEMP% and registered under a randomised Run-key name — is extensively documented as an Agent Tesla delivery and persistence mechanism, as well as MSILHeracles-wrapped loaders. Multiple sandbox contexts confirm the behaviour is reliably reproduced.
string_research.reference: VT sigma_analysis_results (rules: 27b72c26, 8b5db9da, cfe58f03, cf863dff); Joe Sandbox MSILHeracles/AgentTesla report (joesandbox.com/analysis/337706)

string_research.finding: The malware spawns PowerShell to add its own path to Windows Defender exclusions before executing. This is a well-documented anti-AV evasion technique used by Agent Tesla, FormBook, and many .NET-based infostealers/RATs. Sigma rules 7e416af5 and cf863dff both fire on this activity.

Sigma rule (low, rule 0f8e3c8e) fires on file access to Chrome and Edge credential stores. Credential harvesting from browser login databases is a core Agent Tesla capability documented by Palo Alto Unit 42, Malpedia, and MITRE ATT&CK S0331.
string_research.reference: VT sigma_analysis_results; Malpedia win.agent_tesla; MITRE ATT&CK S0331

string_research.finding: Same credential-harvesting pattern as Chrome — Edge login database access. Consistent with Agent Tesla v3+ which targets both Chromium-based browsers.

Sigma rule f4143907 (high) fires on svchost.exe spawned with an uncommon command line, indicating either process hollowing of svchost or a masquerading binary. Process injection/hollowing into svchost is a documented execution technique for Agent Tesla and MSILHeracles-wrapped payloads.
string_research.reference: VT sigma_analysis_results rule f4143907; Joe Sandbox MSILHeracles analysis

string_research.artifact: Jardin de Mousse / Antigravity (Google DeepMind Team) lure strings
string_research.finding: French-language botanical simulation lure. No prior OSINT association. The fabricated 'Google DeepMind' attribution is likely a social-engineering element to lower victim suspicion. No vendor reports reference this specific lure theme.

capabilities: Dropper/loader: unpacks, decrypts, and reflectively loads embedded .NET payload in memory
Persistence: writes randomised Run-key pointing to hidden PowerShell launcher in %TEMP%
Defense evasion: heavy .NET obfuscation (ConfuserEx or equivalent), forged PE timestamp, COMIMAGE_FLAGS_32BITREQUIRED, Windows Defender exclusion via Add-MpPreference
Process injection/hollowing: spawns svchost.exe as a host for injected payload
Browser credential harvesting: Chrome, Edge login databases (via Agent Tesla payload)
Email/FTP client credential harvesting (via Agent Tesla payload)
Data exfiltration via SMTP, HTTP(S), FTP, or Telegram (via Agent Tesla payload)
typical_ttps: T1059.001 — PowerShell (hidden, bypass execution policy, drop and execute PS1)
T1547.001 — Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder (HKCU\Run\<random>)
T1562.001 — Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools (Add-MpPreference ExclusionPath)
T1027 — Obfuscated Files or Information (.NET obfuscation, high-entropy .text)
T1555.003 — Credentials from Web Browsers (Chrome, Edge login data)
associated_actors: Numerous unattributed financially-motivated threat actors — Agent Tesla is commodity crimeware with a leaked builder widely distributed on cybercrime forums
family_intel.behavior_match_with_analysis: Strong agreement: (1) VT Sigma hits confirm PowerShell persistence via Run key, Defender exclusion, svchost hollowing, and browser credential-store access — all documented MSILHeracles/Agent Tesla TTPs; (2) the dominant detection label Gen:Variant.MSILHeracles.250383 across six AV engines with a co-label of AgentTesla (Lionic) and MalwareX-gen [Rat] (AVG/Avast) is consistent with MSILHeracles being the packer/loader layer and Agent Tesla the inner payload; (3) near-maximal .text entropy (7.87) and minimal IAT (only _CorExeMain) align with ConfuserEx-style obfuscation used to protect Agent Tesla loaders; (4) the geolocation-flavoured format strings noted in static analysis are anomalous but may be artefacts of obfuscation or unused lure UI code rather than a separate C2 capability.

actor_intel.actor: Unknown — commodity crimeware; no specific actor attribution possible from available evidence
actor_intel.motivation: Financial — credential theft and data exfiltration for account takeover, fraud, or resale; Agent Tesla is a commodity RAT/stealer available on crimeware markets and via leaked builders
typical_targets: Email credential holders broadly (phishing delivery vector typical)
French-language targets suggested by lure UI (unconfirmed)
prior_campaigns: Agent Tesla has been active since at least 2014 across thousands of campaigns; no specific campaign uniquely identified for this sample
MSILHeracles-wrapped Agent Tesla distribution observed in Joe Sandbox and ANY.RUN public reports (2023–2024)

campaign_links.campaign: MSILHeracles-packed Agent Tesla distribution (generic commodity campaign)
campaign_links.evidence: Dominant AV label Gen:Variant.MSILHeracles.250383 across six major engines; co-label Trojan.Win32.AgentTesla.4!c (Lionic); sandbox behaviour (PowerShell persistence, Defender exclusion, browser credential access, svchost hollowing) matches documented MSILHeracles→AgentTesla kill chain observed in public sandbox reports.

correlations.external_finding: Gen:Variant.MSILHeracles.250383 detected by six AV engines; co-label AgentTesla from Lionic; MalwareX-gen [Rat] from AVG/Avast
correlations.internal_finding: Static analysis: near-maximal .text entropy (7.87), minimal IAT (only _CorExeMain), .NET obfuscation flags, forged 2057 timestamp
correlations.impact: Strongly confirms that the sample is an obfuscated .NET loader/dropper (MSILHeracles layer) wrapping an Agent Tesla infostealer payload. Family confidence raised from 'unknown' to HIGH. The obfuscation observed statically is the defining characteristic of the MSILHeracles packer used to conceal and deliver Agent Tesla.

correlations.external_finding: Sigma rule 27b72c26 (HIGH): New RUN key pointing to suspicious folder — HKCU\Run\qlQjUq pointing to hidden PowerShell + PS1 in %TEMP%
correlations.internal_finding: Static: no static IOCs recovered (obfuscation prevented string extraction); Dynamic: Sigma runtime hits confirm persistence mechanism
correlations.impact: Confirms the malware establishes PowerShell-based persistence via HKCU Run key with randomised name (qlQjUq) — a known Agent Tesla/MSILHeracles persistence pattern. The randomised key name and PS1 filenames suggest automated/templated generation.

correlations.external_finding: Sigma rule 7e416af5 / cf863dff (MEDIUM): Defender exclusion via Add-MpPreference -ExclusionPath
correlations.internal_finding: VT tags: 'detect-debug-environment', 'persistence'; static: COMIMAGE_FLAGS_32BITREQUIRED, no IAT visibility
correlations.impact: Confirms active defense evasion at runtime — the malware adds itself to Defender exclusions before continuing execution, consistent with MSILHeracles loader behaviour documented in sandbox reports.

correlations.external_finding: Sigma rule 0f8e3c8e (LOW): File access to Chrome and Edge login data databases
correlations.internal_finding: Static hypothesis: potential infostealer; Dynamic Sigma hit on credential store file access
correlations.impact: Confirms browser credential harvesting as an active runtime capability, consistent with Agent Tesla's documented credential-theft module (MITRE ATT&CK T1555.003). Validates the 'infostealer' classification.

correlations.external_finding: Sigma rule f4143907 (HIGH): svchost.exe with uncommon command line — potential process hollowing
correlations.internal_finding: Static: no process injection strings visible; Dynamic Sigma hit
correlations.impact: Indicates process injection or hollowing into svchost.exe at runtime — a documented Agent Tesla execution technique used to conceal the payload in a trusted system process.

correlations.external_finding: PE signature_info: product='MothGarden', description='MothGarden', original_name='Unre.exe', copyright='Copyright © 2026'
correlations.internal_finding: Static: French-language botanical lure ('Jardin de Mousse'), 'Antigravity (Google DeepMind Team)' branding, anomalous geolocation format strings
correlations.impact: The fabricated product metadata and lure theme are consistent with social-engineering packaging used to disguise the malware as a legitimate application. No prior OSINT association for 'MothGarden' suggests a novel or custom-built lure, potentially targeting French-speaking users or specific organisations. The fake 'Google DeepMind' attribution adds authority to the lure.

correlations.external_finding: CrowdStrike: win/malicious_confidence_100% (W); SentinelOne: Static AI - Malicious PE; DeepInstinct: MALICIOUS; Elastic: malicious (high confidence)
correlations.internal_finding: Static verdict: low confidence due to obfuscation preventing code analysis
correlations.impact: Multiple ML-based and behaviour-based engines assign 100% or high-confidence malicious verdicts, corroborating the family classification and overcoming the obfuscation barrier that limited static analysis confidence.

correlations.external_finding: First seen in the wild: 2025-06-15; submitted only twice; unique_sources: 2
correlations.internal_finding: Forged PE timestamp (year 2057), near-zero prior OSINT on 'MothGarden' lure string
correlations.impact: Sample is very recent (days old at time of analysis). Low submission count and absence of lure strings in OSINT suggest this is a fresh campaign deployment, possibly targeted. The 34 undetected engines further support novelty.

source_conflicts.conflict: Family label disagreement: majority of engines label the sample MSILHeracles (packer/loader); Lionic labels it AgentTesla (payload); Microsoft labels it Wacatac (generic dropper); AVG/Avast label it MalwareX-gen [Rat]
source_conflicts.resolution: No genuine conflict — MSILHeracles is the packer/loader layer; Agent Tesla is the inner payload. Engines that detect the outer layer label it MSILHeracles; engines that detect the unpacked payload label it AgentTesla. Both detections are likely correct and complementary. Wacatac and generic labels reflect engines that identify the dropper behaviour without classifying the payload.
